speaker: interviewer
text: Today is September 28, 1995. This is an interview with Helen Rosenblatt, maiden name Diament. The interviewer is Martha Frazer. This interview is being conducted in New York, United States of America, in the language English. My name is Martha Frazer. Today is September 28, 1995. I am conducting an interview with Helen Rosenblatt in the Bronx, New York, United States of America. Please state and spell your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Helen-- H-E-L-E-N-- Rosenblatt. R-O-S-E-N-B-L-A-T-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please state and spell your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name at birth was D-I-A-M-E-N-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: At birth was Hela.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: H-E-L-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your birth date and current age?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born July 27, 1912. Right now, I am 83 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please state and spell your city and then the country of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Tschenstochova-- T-S-C-H-E-N-S-T-O-C-H-O-V-A. And it is in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please describe your town for us as it was prior to the Nazi invasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name Tschenstochova hardly appeared on a map. It was a small town. However, everybody in the Catholic world knew about Tschenstochova because there was a shrine of the Madonna. And living in Tschenstochova, for the Jews, was not a simple matter because people used to come with pilgrimage to the place, to the shrine that was called Jasna Gora, a light mountain, and there was the picture of the Madonna. People from Poland used to come, waiting for a miracle at that picture of the Madonna. They came lame and blind. And they hoped that a prayer to Madonna will bring back their health. The lame will walk. And the blind will see. What they got there-- they got the indoctrination by their clergy, who explained to them that all the evil comes from the Jews. So they should hate the Jews. And they promised if a miracle will happen, they will walk back and, on their way, they kill a few Jews. This was the time very hard for us. We had to close the gates because this was the start of a pogrom.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long had your family been living there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They lived since I was born, until the outbreak of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had your parents been born there themselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They came from smaller, little towns around Tschenstochova. At this time, Tschenstochova was meant a big city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please tell us about your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was one of four children. The youngest died, unfortunately, of a typhoid in '35, 1935. The rest, a sister, the oldest sister-- I was the middle one-- and a younger brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was a small business-- had a small business. But he was a person who trusted everyone. And in business, it doesn't pay. Soon he found out that he was cheated. And we were left without an income. My mother was a housewife. She took care of the children and worked at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how many Jewish people were living in Tschenstochova then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know exactly the number. But I think I heard about 12,000. Maybe it's right. Or maybe it's wrong.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the primary occupation of the town catering to these pilgrims? Or were there other industries there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some Jews had business selling some mementos when they came. Others were small stores selling food. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were factories, too. They worked in-- some worked in factories. As far as I remember-- and I belonged to this part of the population-- that we were not rich people. Probably there were some rich people, too. But we had a happy life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the relationship like between the Jewish residents and the non-Jewish residents of the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in a nest of anti-Semitism. To kill a Jew was a big deed-- a mitzvah-- for a Pole. Let me tell you a little-- a little memory when I was seven or eight years old, going to school. And it was winter time, deep snow around. The elementary school was mostly Jewish students. Even the teachers were Jews. When we walked out of the school one day, the Polish guys were waiting for us outdoor, making snowballs, putting a stone inside the snow. And this snowball hit my ear. I had a terrible time, where I remember my mother lit already the candles, because I had to-- soon to die. And a miracle happened. This infection inside my ear, without curing, opened up by itself. And I survived. This was a miracle. And this is a picture how the Poles hated us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a Jewish school that you were going to?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a city school run by Jewish teachers, not exactly a Jewish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's where you went for elementary school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Elementary school. Now, after I finished elementary school, I wanted to continue my education. I had a hunger for knowledge. The high school was private. We have to pay tuition. At this time, this economic situation at home was very bad. There was no money for my education. So over the summer, before I entered the high school, I tutored little children. I was then 12 years old. And I earned some money. The rest came by scholarship. And I attended a Hebrew high school, where I got the background of Zionism, Jewish culture, and language. After graduation from high school, my parents said, that's it. I could not agree. I wanted to go to college. In our town, there was no college. The closest one was in Krakow, a famous old university with a big reputation. And I wanted to go there. I found, luckily, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some relatives of my friends who lived in Krakow and had little children. And for room and board-- room and board I got for helping the children, taking care of the children. I enrolled in the University of Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a quota system as to how many Jews could enroll?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jews could enroll only for philosophy and for languages. Medicine was out of question. Law-- partially. I studied at the time of [NON-ENGLISH]. It means, no way for Jews. And when I came for classes, I had to watch out for those Polish guys who wanted to hurt us. Changing-- standing for all the lectures, we were not allowed to sit down. We stood on the left side. We were second rate citizen. We didn't have the right. I took notes standing. But sometimes I have to change classes. When we walked from one room to the other, we, we found in the lobby the Polish guys standing with baseball, baseball bats and hit us over our heads. This does not disturb me to continue and to achieve my goal. I graduated summa cum laude with a big, big thank you from my professor. I still remember him. He was not Polish. He came from Yugoslavia. His name was Vukadunovicz. I turned to him for a job after the graduation. And this what he said. I'm so sorry. This is against the policy. I cannot provide you. I cannot find a job for you. I did my experience for one year in a high school, without pay, and returned to Tschenstochova. Luckily, there was a Jewish private school, and I was accepted as a teacher. I was teaching. This is-- this was an elementary school, but a warm, beautiful environment. And I enjoyed teaching in this school. By the way, I found one of my students who survived as a Holocaust survivor in Newark. And she still remembers me. I did something right. This was until 1935. I did-- I was not satisfied with my MA in languages. I majored in German literature, and my minor was Latin. I wanted to go on and have a doctorate. I wrote my thesis in Tschenstochova, sometimes traveling to Krakow to get some material needed. I finished my thesis. I delivered it. However, the war broke out, and this was the end of looking for my doctorate.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were at the university, about how many other Jewish students were with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of them. Jews had the hunger to go to higher education.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in a class, how many people would be standing on the left side of, generally, in a class?

speaker: interviewer
text: That many?

speaker: interviewer
text: About 20 students. That's remarkable.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe more. Some classes, more. Some classes, less. But we had a determination to go on, even so that it was very, very hard. When we came in the morning to the university and saw a sign-- a day without Jews-- hurts. We went through the back door, but we continued.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were teaching in Tschenstochova from about '35 to 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your brother or sister go on for any further education?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister didn't go to high school. Instead, she went to be a nurse. So she worked with the hospital. My brother learned a trade [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] making furniture. But he was young at this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any sign that war might be coming? Were people concerned about what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We heard from outside, something is going to happen. But being young, and being at this time in love, I didn't see it. I said, that's too far away. And as the summer before September 9, 1939 came, this was a very happy summer for me. I started ready to make preparations for some project in school. I just said, what outside? It doesn't bother me. I had great hopes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned that you were in love at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your boyfriend do? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: He finished high school, but didn't go on. He was born the same day, the same year, exactly. And we were 16 and in love. We didn't see any hardship from outside. He then became my husband. Later on, I, I return to this story.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Polish, he was Romek. But his Hebrew name was Avraham. His last name was Szczupak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-Z-C-Z-U-P-A-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you hear about the war starting? What was the first thing that affected you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard from outside that the German are coming to Poland to sell Poland away. And this happened in the history of Poland many times. So we did not pay attention to it until the day of this September 1, 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: September 1, 1939, the war broke out. Hitler's mighty army attacked Poland. All plans shattered. This serious situation undermined my great spirit. I was scared. The constant wailing of sirens, flying bombs, and fires created panic. We had to run to shelters in the basement, joining the other families. My mother kept us calm with her constant assurance that the great commotion is only a military exercise of the Polish Air Force. She was so brave, a real heroine. After a few days, the constant bombardment came to a stop. The Polish army, defeated, was fleeing to the east, and with them, thousands of helpless civilians. My sister, Lucy, and her family were on vacation when the war broke out. They joined the other civilians fleeing behind the army. Only after weeks, we found out that they are in the east, Baranowicze, safe, but without any means of survival.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in her family at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was there with her little girl of two and her husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when the Germans invaded, at that time, did the Jewish people in your town have an idea of how the Jews in Germany had been treated or what might be ha-- going-- in store?

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard already about families who came from Germany because it was an expulsion of Jews from Germany. They came in their nightgowns, in their robes, chased out by Hitler. It was already since 1933. We tried to accept those people and hope that this happened only where Hitler is, but he will not be able to come where we are. My first encounter with reality was the day I saw German soldiers seeking for water. They ordered me to drink first to make sure it's not poisoned. On the street, I saw dead bodies, rows of rows of captured prisoners and mighty tanks. I was scared and ran home. Here, I was safe. I ha-- I had my parents, my loving home. Food was scarce, but we managed. And now, gray days followed with no hope for the better. And then the first order of the day-- Jews must wear the yellow sign, register for work, give away fur-- furs and jewelry.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how long after the Germans took over did this order come?

speaker: interviewee
text: A couple of months.

speaker: interviewer
text: About two months?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish committee was responsible for supplying enough workers. My father was too fragile for hard labor. I was fortunate to have Mr. Weinreb, a friend of my family who was in the committee. He found a way to free my father from this order. I personally was employed-- forced labor. No pay.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was the job that you were assigned to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a cleaning woman at the private quarters of the [GERMAN]. My brother, a teenager, had to work as a slave. The general atmosphere in Jewish circles was depressing. Some had tried to return to regular life. Despite the hard times, I was lucky to have my loving family, my love. The news from the front were a little encouraging. The German had stopped this conquest at the Vistula River, facing the mighty Russian army. This, at the same time, a sweet surprise warmed our hearts. My sister Lucy appeared home. It was hard to believe her bravery. In very cold weather, she smuggled through Baranowizce-- what was under the Russian far in the east. To break through the lines of the fighting armies was so dangerous. The need to help her young child and her husband gave her courage to get home and bring warm clothes, food, and money. How we worried about her return trip. She had made it safely. Back to our daily troubles and living under the watchful eye of oppressors. The German plan was to destroy the Jews. They did it very systematically. First, break their spirit. Break their souls in fate. Bring them to despair. Then starve them and kill.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, your sister went back to Russia. Was she able to get word to you that she was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or you didn't find that out till the war was over?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We tried to send letters. I don't know if she ever received it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you never heard from her?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. At this point, I am coming to November the 9th, the Kristallnacht. So if you want to ask me something--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So--

speaker: interviewee
text: Or should I--

speaker: interviewer
text: When she returned, that was before November?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was shortly after the occupation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So then, in November, the Germans enacted more anti-Jewish laws. And would you tell us about what happened in your town then, November 9th? Yeah. First, on November 9th, the well-known Kristallnacht, this was the first step to destroy the Jews. It was a cold, rainy morning. We wrote up-- we woke up in panic-- all around us flames, fires. We ran to the backyard and stood in silence. What happened? Soon, we found out the truth. All homes of worship, gone up in flames. About how many synagogues were there in your town?

speaker: interviewer
text: Helen, it's late.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how many synagogues were there?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. That's enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hardly can count it.
